coredata.dc:title: Preparation of a Z-system photocatalyst (oxygen-doped carbon nitride/nitrogen-doped carbon dots/bismuth tetroxide) and its application in a photocatalytic fuel cell
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Hongbin
Xue, Yanan
Liang, Shuang
Wang, Xinhong
coredata.dc:description: Oxygen-doped carbon nitride (OPCN) and bismuth tetroxide (Bi2O4) nano-rods were combined to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst (OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as electron transfer mediators. The physicochemical properties of the materials and their photocatalytic performance were investigated in details. The results indicated that OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation rate of tetracycline over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 increased by 34.6% and 37.3%, respectively, compared with that over OPCN/NCDs and Bi2O4 alone (as for methyl orange, the degradation rate increased by 1125% and 58.3%, respectively). A possible Z-scheme mechanism was proposed. With the help of the Z-scheme structure of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers was effectively inhibited, and their high redox ability could be simultaneously retained. Consequently, the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants with respect to activity and stability was enhanced significantly. In addition, with this Z-scheme photocatalyst as the photoanode to construct a photocatalytic fuel cell, the performance of decontamination and power generation could be also improved. This work provides an idea to design visible-light-responded photocatalysts for wastewater treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: O-doped g-C3N4
N-doped carbon dots
Bi2O4
Z-scheme
Photocatalytic fuel cell

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Degradation of pollutants by photocatalysis or PFC
Characterization of photocatalysts using XRD, EDX and XPS

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51878133).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (a) OPCN (inset is the HRTEM image of OPCN/NCDs), (b) Bi2O4, (c) OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, and (d) the TEM image of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 (inset is its HRTEM image).
XRD patterns of the as-prepared photocatalysts.
XPS spectra of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 (a) survey, (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) O 1 s and (e) Bi 4f.
Transient photocurrent responses (a) and electrochemical impedance spectra (b) of different photocatalysts.
Photocatalytic degradation of TC (a) and MO (c) over different photocatalysts, and the corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetics constants (b and d).
DRS spectra of different photocatalysts (a) and Tauc’s plots (b) and Mott-Schottky plots of OPCN (c) and Bi2O4 (d).
Scavenger tests for the degradation of TC over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4.
J-V curves (a) and P-V curves (b) of the PFC systems with different photoanodes.
J-V curves and P-V curves of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 PFC with the addition of different fuels.

head.ce:title: Preparation of a Z-system photocatalyst (oxygen-doped carbon nitride/nitrogen-doped carbon dots/bismuth tetroxide) and its application in a photocatalytic fuel cell

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Plant Protection, Jilin Agricultural University, Changchun 130118, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Plant Protection, Jilin Agricultural University, Changchun 130118, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environment, Northeast Normal University, Changchun 130117, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxygen-doped carbon nitride (OPCN) and bismuth tetroxide (Bi2O4) nano-rods were combined to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst (OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4) with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as electron transfer mediators. The physicochemical properties of the materials and their photocatalytic performance were investigated in details. The results indicated that OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 exhibited the highest photocatalytic activity under visible light irradiation. The photocatalytic degradation rate of tetracycline over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 increased by 34.6% and 37.3%, respectively, compared with that over OPCN/NCDs and Bi2O4 alone (as for methyl orange, the degradation rate increased by 1125% and 58.3%, respectively). A possible Z-scheme mechanism was proposed. With the help of the Z-scheme structure of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, the recombination of photo-generated charge carriers was effectively inhibited, and their high redox ability could be simultaneously retained. Consequently, the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants with respect to activity and stability was enhanced significantly. In addition, with this Z-scheme photocatalyst as the photoanode to construct a photocatalytic fuel cell, the performance of decontamination and power generation could be also improved. This work provides an idea to design visible-light-responded photocatalysts for wastewater treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: The environmental pollution and energy crisis have been the serious problems that mankind is facing today. In recent years, the photocatalytic technology has been proved to be a promising method for eliminating pollutants and recovering renewable fuels and energy. A large number of photocatalysts have been explored, such as graphene-based semiconductors [1], halides and oxyhalides-based materials [2], graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [3], and CdS [4]. They have been applied to the photocatalytic reduction of CO2 into fuels [5], hydrogen production [6], pollutant elimination [7], etc.

Among these materials, g-C3N4 is an excellent semiconductor material with many unique characteristics, e.g. nonmetal and high chemical stability, and has been widely used in different photocatalytic processes [8–10]. However, the photocatalytic performance of the bulk g-C3N4 is not good due to its poor visible light response, small specific surface area and high recombination of photo-excited charge carriers [11,12]. So far, different strategies have been tried to improve its photocatalytic performance, including metal [13,14] or nonmetal [15,16] doping, co-catalyst surface modification [17,18], and combination with other semiconductor materials to construct heterojunctions [19,20]. Unfortunately, these methods were expensive and complex. Recently, it was reported that an effectively visible-light-driven oxygen-doped porous g-C3N4 (OPCN) could be synthesized by a facile thermal copolymerization method, and exhibited a higher photocatalytic activity than the pristine g-C3N4 [21].

Additionally, as environment-friendly materials, bismuth-based semiconductors, such as BiOBr [22], BiVO4 [23] and Bi2Mo6 [24], have been also paidmuchattention by researchers. It was reported that the empty 6 s orbital of Bi5+ ions in bismuth-based compounds could modify their band gap and support higher visible photocatalytic activity [25,26]. For example, Bi2O4 has a narrow band gap of around 1.6 eV due to the hybrid valence states of Bi3+ and Bi5+. The Bi2O4-based photocatalysts showed promising abilities for antibacterial [27], nitrogen fixation [28], and pollutant removal [29], etc. Nevertheless, Bi2O4 was not stable especially under irradiation for long time because the high-valence bismuth (Bi5+) in Bi2O4 would change into the lower one (Bi3+) [30]. Besides, the photocatalytic activity of Bi2O4 alone was usually not very high because of the rapid recombination of photo-excited charge carriers [28,29]. Therefore, more efforts should be made to overcome these shortcomings.

An effective approach was to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction, with which the photo-generated electrons and holes could transfer in two photo-systems, facilitating their high efficient separation. Simultaneously, the electrons with strong reduction ability in higher conduction band (CB) and the holes with strong oxidation ability in lower valence band (VB) could be reserved to enhance the photocatalytic activity [31]. Consequently, some Z-scheme photocatalysts were prepared by using g-C3N4 and Bi2O4, and the improved photocatalytic performance could be usually obtained with respect to disinfection [32] and pollutant elimination [33,34]. Despite the fact that a great effort has been made in designing the heterojunction between g-C3N4 and Bi2O4, it remained challenging to improve electron transfer at the interface and enhance light absorption capacity of g-C3N4. As mentioned above, oxygen doping (O-doping) was an effective method to extend the light response of g-C3N4 to longer wavelength region [21]. Additionally, in many heterojunction materials, N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were considered as good electron transfer mediators that could assist the Z-scheme heterojunction construction and enhance the photocatalytic performance [35,36]. NCD is a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial with many outstanding properties, e.g. excellent electron transfer and electron reservoir properties, and up-conversion fluorescence behavior [37,38]. Therefore, it is possible to get a good photocatalytic activity by combining OPCN wtih Bi2O4 to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst wtih NCDs as electron transfer mediators.

In this paper, a new solid-state Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst (OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4) was prepared. The photocatalytic activity was evaluated by the degradation of tetracycline (TC) and methyl orange (MO) under visible light irradiation. The possible photocatalytic mechanism was proposed. Besides, with OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 as the photoanode, a photocatalytic fuel cell (PFC) was constructed, and its performance for electricity generation and pollutant elimination was investigated. As reported in literatures [39,40], the PFC system seemed an attractive technology that could provide an alternative source of renewable energy as well as great environmental benefits.

NCDs were synthesized by a hydrothermal method [3]. Briefly, 3.0 g of citric acid and 1.0 g of urea were dissolved in 15 mL deionized water and stirred for 1 h. The mixture was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min, and then dried at 70 °C overnight. Finally, the NCD powder was obtained.

A certain amount of urea and ammonium oxalate (mass ratio 1:0.09) were dissolved in deionized water, and 1 wt% of NCDs was dispersed into the above solution and stirred at room temperature for 1 h. Subsequently, the mixture was vaporized to dryness at 70 °C. After being ground, the obtained powder was calcined in a ceramic crucible with a lid at 550 °C for 2.5 h. The pristine OPCN was prepared by the same route but in absence of NCDs [21].

The composite OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 was prepared by a hydrothermal method. Different amounts of OPCN/NCDs were added into 0.01 M NaBiO3 solution and stirred for 30 min to obtain a uniform suspension. The suspension was then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 8 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the mixture was centrifuged and washed with ethanol and deionized water successively for several times and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. The mass ratios of Bi2O4 to OPCN/NCDs were set as 1:1, 2:1, 4:1, and 8:1. The pristine Bi2O4 was prepared under the same hydrothermal conditions without the addition of OPCN/NCDs [32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Degradation of pollutants by photocatalysis or PFC
ce:section.ce:para: The visible light was provided by a 300 W Xe lamp (CEL-HXF 300) equipped with a UV cut-off filter. The filter is a longpass filter that can allow the light with wavelength longer than 420 nm to pass. The incident radiation intensity was 150 mW·cm−2. The photocatalytic experiments were carried out in a quartz vessel containing 100 mL of TC or MO (10 mg/L) and 0.05 g of photocatalysts. Before illumination, the suspension was stirred in dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption–desorption equilibrium. During photocatalysis, a certain volume of aliquots were sampled at given time intervals to detect the concentration of TC or MO by a UV–vis spectrophotometer. As for the PFC system, the illumination area of photoanodes and the Cu2O/FTO photocathode was 2 cm2. The photoanodes were prepared by a drop-coating method. Typically, 40 mg of the photocatalysts was ultrasonically dispersed in a mixed solution containing 800 μL ethanol and 50 μL Nafion for 20 min. A pre-cleaned FTO substrate was coated with this mixture by drop-coating, and then dried at 120 °C for 1 h in the nitrogen atmosphere. The Cu2O/FTO photocathode was synthesized by an electro-deposition method [31]. Briefly, with a FTO substrate, a Pt foil and SCE as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode, respectively, the Cu2O/FTO photocathode was prepared by electro-deposition at −0.6 V vs. SCE for 10 min at 50 °C under magnetically stirring. A solution containing 0.1 M CuSO4 and 0.75 M lactic acid was used as the electrolyte.

The materials were listed in the Supporting Information. The characterizations were carried out as follows. The morphology was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JSM-7500F, Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Philips CM200). The crystal structure and surface elements were analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα irradiation (D/maxZ200PC, Rigaku, Japan) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with Al Kα radiation (ESCALAB MK II, VG Scientific, England), respectively. The UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were recorded on a UV–Vis-NIR Spectrophotometer (TU-1950 spectrophotometer). Electrochemical characterizations were carried out on an electrochemical working station (CHI 760E, China) in a standard three electrode system with SCE as the reference electrode.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, the as-prepared OPCN was irregular and porous. The HRTEM image of OPCN/NCDs (inset of Fig. 1a) showed that the diameter of NCDs was ca. 5 nm, and the inter-planar spacing of 0.22 nm could be assigned to the (100) facet of graphitic carbon [3,37]. Nevertheless, the lattice fringe information of OPCN was not illustrated clearly possibly due to its low crystallinity. It could be seen from Fig. 1b that the pristine Bi2O4 mainly appeared as nano-rods with the length of 1 μm and the diameter of 100 nm. The morphologies of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 were shown in Fig. 1c (SEM) and Fig. 1d (TEM). It could be seen that Bi2O4 nano-rods closely contacted with OPCN. The lattice fringe of 0.29 nm (inset of Fig. 1d) was consistent with the lattice spacing of (400) planes of Bi2O4 [33,34]. Additionally, the SEM image of Cu2O was shown in Fig. S1. It could be seen that many particles with the diameter of 30 nm agglomerated together.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of photocatalysts using XRD, EDX and XPS
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 showed the XRD patterns of g-C3N4, OPCN, OPCN/NCDs, Bi2O4, and OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4. As for g-C3N4, there were two characteristic peaks at 13.1° and 27.6°, corresponding to (100) and (002) crystal planes, respectively. The strong (002) peak should be ascribed to the characteristic interlayer stacking structure of aromatic segments [3]. By O-doping, the (002) diffraction peak of OPCN slightly shifted to lower diffraction angles, and the peakwasbroadened (as shown in the inset). This was because of the increased interplanar stacking distance and the decreased long-range order of graphite stacking caused by O-doping [21]. The modification of NCDs gave rise to no obvious changes in the diffraction peaks of OPCN. However, no diffraction information on NCDs was observed due to their small quantity and well dispersion, which was similar to what reported in literatures [3]. The diffraction peaks of the pristine Bi2O4 could be indexed to the monoclinic phase (JCPDS #50–0864) [28,29]. The strong intensity of diffraction peaks implied its well crystal structure [4]. When Bi2O4 hybridized with OPCN/NCDs, the main diffraction peaks of Bi2O4 did not change in diffraction angle, but the intensity decreased slightly, indicating their well combination. The (002) peak of OPCN was not clearly identifiable, which was attributed to the peak position overlap between OPCN and Bi2O4.

The EDS mapping and XPS with high-resolution analysis were carried out to identify the elemental composition and chemical states of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4. As shown in Fig. S2, the elements of C, N, O and Bi were recorded in the EDS spectrum, revealing no impurities were in OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4. The EDS mapping of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 further confirmed the existence of these four elements. The uniform distribution of C, N, O and Bi suggested that the composite OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 was successfully synthesized. The results of XPS characterization were shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a showed the survey spectrum of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, in which C, N, O, and Bi were detected. As shown in Fig. 3b, four types of carbon could be deconvoluted from the C1s spectra. The peaks at 284.7 eV, 286.4 eV and 288.3 eV were ascribed to adventitious carbon, sp2 carbon of the C–NH2 group and sp2-hybridized carbon atoms of triazine rings (N–C = N), respectively. Besides, a new peak at 289.1 eV was assigned to the O-C-N bond in OPCN, suggesting that O atoms were successfully doped by replacing N atoms [21,41,42]. The high resolution N1s spectra presented in Fig. 3c could be fitted into three peaks centered at 398.6 eV, 400.2 eV and 401.3 eV, corresponding to different types nitrogen of C–N = C, N–(C)3 and C–N–H, respectively [21,41,42]. The O1s XPS spectra could be deconvoluted into three peaks (Fig. 3d). The peak at 529.2 eV was attributed to the lattice oxygen (Bi-O) [30], while the one at 530.5 eV could be assigned to the C–O and N–C–O bonds in the crystal lattice of OPCN, revealing that O was successfully introduced in OPCN [41]. The peak at 532.3 eV was ascribed to the hydroxyl oxygen or adsorbed water [30,34,41]. Fig. 3e showed the Bi4f spectra. The strong peak of Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2 could be divided into two bimodal peaks at binding energies of 158.2 and 158.8 eV, and of 163.5 and 164.1 eV, which were assigned to Bi(III) and Bi(V) of Bi2O4, respectively [32,33].

The photocatalytic activity was evaluated by degrading model pollutants or carrying out photoelectrochemical tests. Firstly, testing transient photocurrent responses was an effective method to clarify the performance of charge transfer and the recombination of photo-generated carriers. This was because the photocatalytic activity of catalysts was directly influenced by the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs. As shown in Fig. 4 a, OPCN showed a small photocurrent response because of its highest resistance, which could be confirmed by the following EIS analysis (Fig. 4b). After modification with NCDs, the photocurrent response increased a little. The enhanced photocurrent response might be attributed to the fact that NCDs were good electron acceptors, and could efficiently capture the photo-generated electrons from OPCN, thus improving the separation of charge carriers and electron transfer [43] (the resistance of OPCN/NCDs was indeed decreased, as illustrated in Fig. 4b). The other possible reason was that the remarkable up-conversion fluorescent property of NCDs allowed itself an enhanced light-harvesting ability [44]. Bi2O4 presented a relatively high photocurrent response, and the photocurrent intensity increased further once Bi2O4 was combined with OPCN/NCDs. In the composite OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, NCDs could not only act as the electron acceptors, but also help to construct a heterojunction between OPCN and Bi2O4, and facilitate the separation of charge carriers. The mechanism was detailed analyzed in the following sections. Normally, a smaller semicircle for the impedance arc in EIS spectra meant a lower charge transfer resistance at the contact interface between the electrode and electrolyte solution [6,9]. It could be seen form Fig. 4b and Fig. S3 that, compared with other photocatalysts, OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 exhibited the smallest arc radius and the lowest charge-transfer resistance (Rct), indicating its best electrochemical property for charge transport. This might contribute to a better separation efficiency of the photo-generated charge carriers and improved photocatalytic activity of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 [6]. The results were well consistent with the findings of photocurrent tests.

Under visible light irradiation (>420 nm), the photocatalytic degradation of model pollutants (TC and MO) was carried out over different photocatalysts, i.e. the pristine g-C3N4, OPCN, OPCN/NCDs, Bi2O4 and OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, TC was not efficiently degraded by the pristine g-C3N4, while the pseudo-first-order kinetics constant of OPCN increased by 111%. As reported in literatures [21], O-doping was an effective approach to boost the photocatalytic activity of carbon nitride. After modification with NCDs, the kinetics constant became further larger due to the high separation efficiency of charge carriers with the help of NCDs. In order to obtain a better photocatalytic activity, the ternary composite OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 was constructed (the mass ratios of Bi2O4 to OPCN/NCDs were set as 1:1, 2:1, 4:1, and 8:1). As expected, the ternary composite (mass ratio 4:1) displayed the best photocatalytic performance, and the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 increased by 34.6% and 37.3%, respectively, compared with that over OPCN/NCDs or Bi2O4 alone. Similar results were obtained for MO (Fig. 5c). The degradation rate of MO over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 was 11.25 and 0.583 times faster than that over OPCN/NCDs and Bi2O4 alone, respectively (Fig. 5d). The significantly improved photocatalytic performance should be ascribed to the formation of the Z-scheme heterjunction between OPCN and Bi2O4 with NCDs as the electron transfer mediators. With the help of the Z-scheme structure, high efficient separation of photo-generated charge carriers could be realized [37,38] with the strong redox ability being reserved [36]. Moreover, NCDs could enhance the light harvesting capacity and the molecular oxygen activation ability of the photocatalyst [35]. All of these properties contributed to a good photocatalytic performance. In order to clarify the mineralization degree of pollutants, the TOC values were determined. The results indicated that the TOC removal efficiencies for TC and MO over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 were 48.7 ± 3.4% and 33.2 ± 4.0%, respectively. This implied that, although the parent compounds of TC and MO could be degraded efficiently, it should take longer time to achieve complete mineralization. In addition, the reusability of the photocatalysts, i.e. OPCN/NCDs, Bi2O4 and OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, was investigated by degrading TC for three cycles. As shown in Fig. S4, the degradation efficiency over Bi2O4 decreased dramatically from 80.6% to 50.2% after three cycles. In contrast, the stability of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 and OPCN/NCDs was relatively better. It was believed that the reduction of high-valence bismuth in Bi2O4 to Bi3+ under illumination was the main reason for the deteriorated photocatalytic performance of Bi2O4 after recycling runs [30]. Therefore, the Z-scheme structure of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 could not only boost its photocatalytic performance, but also improve its photochemical stability.

As we know, in the actual wastewater treatment process, the operating conditions may be in dynamic variation. Therefore, it is necessary to investigate the photocatalytic performance under different conditions, i.e. pH, the initial concentration of pollutants and the dosage of photocatalyts. In this part of experiments, TC was used as the model pollutant. Firstly, a series of experiments were conducted by adjusting the initial solution pH at 3.0, 5.3, 7.0 and 9.0. It could be seen from Fig. S5a that the degradation efficiency of TC decreased slightly with the increase in the pH value. Although a lower pH value was favorable for the photocatalytic degradation of TC in this work, the pH 5.3 might be more suitable because the as-prepared TC solution pH was around 5.3. It was undoubtedly more reasonable to directly eliminate pollutants without need of adjusting the solution pH. This could simplify the treating process and save operation cost. However, to clarify the specific function of pH was a really tough job. This was because the solution pH could influence many factors, such as the surface charge of photocatalysts, the adsorption and dissociation of substrates, and the generation of reactive oxygen species [18,20]. In fact, as far as the TC degradation was concerned, the optimal pH was usually not the same in different works. It was reported that a higher pH was more suitable [7,18], whereas the neutral condition was also favorable in other cases [43]. Consequently, the solution pH played variant roles in different photocatalytic systems. In view of this, it is very necessary to explore the effect of pH on the pollutant removal in the practical wastewater treatment by considering both the degradation efficiency and the operation cost. Secondly, the effect of initial TC concentrations (5–40 mg/L) was investigated. As seen in Fig. S5b, the degradation efficiency of TC gradually decreased as its initial concentration increased. Similar trends were observed in different photocatalytic systems with different model pollutants [7,18,26,37,43]. This phenomenon was possibly owing to three reasons: (i) the surface reactive sites were limited [37]; (ii) high concentration of organic compounds prevented photocatalysts from effectively absorbing photons [18,26]; (iii) the intermediates competed with mother molecules for the limited reactive sites [7]. Thirdly, the effect of the catalyst dosage was also examined due to the fact that the catalyst dosage could definitely influence the operation cost in advanced oxidation processes. As shown in Fig. S5c, the degradation efficiency enhanced greatly when the catalyst dosage increased from 20 mg/L to 50 mg/L, but the degradation of TC was not improved significantly when more catalysts were used. The increased degradation efficiency with the increase in the catalyst dosage was mainly because the more photocatalysts were used, the more reactive sites could be provided. However, a higher dosage than 100 mg/L was not investigated because excessive catalysts might result in higher turbidity, shielding light absorption and thus deteriorating the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants [26,37,43]. Moreover, considering the operation cost, a higher catalyst dosage may not be a better choice.

The light absorption capacity and the band structures of different photocatalysts were characterized by DRS and Mott-Schottky tests. It could be seen from Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b that the absorption edge of the pristine g-C3N4 was at around 420 nm, and the corresponding band gap (Eg) was near to 3.0 eV. O-doping significantly enhanced the light absorption capacity of OPCN. An obvious red-shift was obtained. The Eg decreased to 2.16 eV. After modification with NCDs, the light absorption property of OPCN/NCDs did not present obvious changes because the NCDs loading was relatively low although its light absorption capacity was very high in the whole range from 200 nm to 800 nm (Fig. 6a). Bi2O4 had a wider absorption range and a smaller Eg of 1.96 eV. Generally, a narrower Eg could usually give rise to a better photocatalytic performance due to the enhancement of visible light absorption.

The flat band potential (Vfb) was determined by testing Mott-Schottky plots. As illustrated in Fig. 6c and Fig. 6d, the slopes of OPCN and Bi2O4 were positive, indicating their n-type characteristics. According to the intercepts of Mott-Schottky plots, the Vfb values of OPCN and Bi2O4 were −1.10 eV and −0.46 eV (vs. SCE), respectively, corresponding to −0.86 eV and −0.22 eV (vs. NHE, NHE = SCE + 0.24 V). For a n-type semiconductor, the conduction band (ECB) was generally estimated to be 0.1 eV more negative than Vfb [30], while the valence band (EVB) could be calculated according to the empirical equation EVB = Eg + ECB. Consequently, the ECB potentials of OPCN and Bi2O4 were −0.96 eV and −0.32 eV (vs. NHE), respectively, and the corresponding EVB potentials were + 1.2 eV and + 1.64 eV (vs. NHE).

In order to clarify the photocatalytic mechanism over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, the scavenger tests were carried out using isopropanol (IPA), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) and benzoquinone (BQ) as the scavengers for ·OH, h+ and ·O2 −, respectively. The results were shown in Fig. 7 . The photocatalytic degradation of TC was greatly inhibited only when BQ was added, suggesting that ·O2 − was the primary reactive species. The addition of Na2C2O4 gave rise to a moderate inhibition, which indicated that h+ played the secondary role in the photocatalytic process. However, IPA just had a small impact on the TC removal. So ·OH only contributed slightly to the TC degradation. In conclusion, in this photocatalytic system, ∙O2 – was the dominating reactive species, followed by h+ and then ·OH. The results observed here were similar to the findings reported in literatures [32–34].

Based on the band structure analysis and the trapping experiments mentioned above, a Z-scheme mechanism was proposed for the photocatalytic process over OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4. Firstly, considering the ECB of OPCN (-0.96 eV vs. NHE) and Bi2O4 (-0.32 eV vs. NHE), ·O2 − should be mainly generated through the reduction of O2 by the photo-generated electrons on the CB of g-C3N4, but not Bi2O4. This was because the CB potential of Bi2O4 was more positive than the standard redox potential of ·O2 −/O2 (-0.33 eV vs. NHE) [33]. It was reported that only holes of Bi2O4 did work in the photocatalytic process [32]. The holes of both OPCN and Bi2O4 might be able to participate in the photocatalytic degradation of pollutants. However, it was thermodynamically forbidden to directly produce ·OH through oxidizing water or OH− by holes of OPCN and Bi2O4. This was due to the fact that the VB potentials of both OPCN and Bi2O4 were more negative than that of ·OH/OH− (+1.99 eV vs. NHE). Therefore, asmallamountof ·OH observed in the trapping experiments should be mainly produced from multistep reactions via ·O2 − [32]. According to the above theoretical and experimental analysis, the mechanism of the Z-scheme heterojunction, instead of the conventional type II heterojunction, was proposed and schematically demonstrated in Fig. 8 . This mechanism has been normally accepted by other researchers [32–34]. Under visible light irradiation, both OPCN and Bi2O4 could be excited to produce electrons and holes owing to their narrow band gaps. Due to the small size and superior electron conductivity of NCDs, they could be used as the electron transfer mediators to bridge the electrons on the CB of Bi2O4 to the VB of OPCN. This could not only promote efficient separation of charge carriers, but also reserve their strong redox capacities, thus leading to a high photocatalytic activity [35–38]. Instead, if the charge transfer at interfaces followed the conventional type II transfer mode, electrons would accumulate on the CB of Bi2O4. As discussed above, it was not favorable to thermodynamically produce ·O2 −/O2 by Bi2O4. Therefore, the dominating reactive species should be h+, but not ·O2 −, which was inconsistent with the experimental result.

The photocatalytic performance of the PFC systems with different photoanodes was investigated by degrading MO under visible light irradiation. As illustrated in Fig. S6, the removal efficiency of MO was only approximate 20% in the PFC systems with OPCN and OPCN/NCDs as the photoanodes. Bi2O4 had a relatively high activity, and 53% of MO could be removed in 240 min. A further enhanced degradation efficiency was obtained in the Z-scheme PFC system (OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4). This phenomenon should be attributed to the high separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers with the help of the unique Z-scheme structure of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4. The results were well consistent with what observed in Fig. 5, in which the powder photocatalysts were used.

Fig. 9 a and Fig. 9b showed the current–voltage (J-V) and power-voltage (P-V) plots recorded in the PFC systems with different photoanodes. The photovoltaic parameters were summarized in Table S1. As previously reported [31], the internal resistance (R int) of photoanodes hadagreatimpacton the PFC performance. A lower R int usually gave rise to a better charge transfer property, leading to a better PFC performance in the aspect of the open circuit potential (V OC). As shown in Table S1, the Z-scheme PFC system exhibited the largest V OC because of the smallest resistance of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 (confirmed by the EIS result, Fig. 4b). As illustrated in Fig. 9b and Table S1, the maximum power density (P max) of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 PFC was 21.36 μW/cm2, which was 3.13, 7.29, and 15.15 times that of Bi2O4 PFC (6.82 μW/cm2), OPCN/NCDs PFC (2.93 μW/cm2), and OPCN PFC (1.41 μW/cm2), respectively. The electricity generation performance was well consist with the degradation results (Fig. S6). As discussed above, the electrical conductivity of electrodes should be one of the factors to influence the PFC performance. Besides, a higher separation efficiency of photo-generated charge carriers was also an favorable property to enhance P max. With the help of the Z-scheme heterojunction of OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4, the recombination of photo-generated carriers could be effectively inhibited. Thus, more photo-excited electrons would be generated and transfer to the cathode, leading to a better electricity production.

Besides the intrinsic properties of photo-electrodes, e.g. band structure, separation of photo-generated charge carriers and internal resistance, operating conditions might also influence the PFC performance. Firstly, three refractory organic compounds, i.e. MO, TC and 4-chlorophenol, were selected as the fuels to investigate the effect of fuels on the PFC performance. It could be seen from Fig. 10 and Table S2 that the PFC performance of electricity production was significantly influenced by the added substrate species. The short-circuit current density (J SC) and P max varied much with each other. This was possibly because the degradability of different organic compounds with various molecular structures were different [45,46]. Additionally, the phenomenon of photosensitization should not be ignored especially for dye fuels, which could inject more electrons into the photoanode and enhance electricity production [47,48]. As observed in the experiments, the PFC system with MO as the fuel showed the best performance.

The effect of the initial MO concentrations (from 2 to 20 mg/) on the electricity generation of PFC was investigated. As shown in Fig. S7, the highest current density and the largest power output were obtained at the initial MO concentration of 5 mg/L. Under visible light irradiation, organic compounds could be oxidized on the photoanode, facilitating more free electrons to transfer to the cathode, and thus generating electricity. Nevertheless, higher MO concentrations than 5 mg/L deteriorated the PFC performance instead. Similar phenomena were also observed in literatures [48,49]. This was because the limited photoanode surface could not provide enough reactive sites. Furthermore, the higher the MO concentration was, the darker the solution color was. This weakened the light transmission, preventing the photoanode from being effectively excited.

The solution pH played an important role for the PFC system. A lower pH value (or higher concentration of H+) was normally helpful for oxygen reduction and hydrogen evolution on the cathode, thus facilitating electrons to transfer from the photoanode to the cathode [49]. Besides, the solution pH could also influence the generation of reactive oxygen species and the adsorption–desorption of substrates on electrodes [49,50]. Therefore, the impact of pH on the PFC performance should be multifaceted. As illustrated in Fig. S8, the PFC performance at pH 3.5 was obviously better that at neutral or alkaline conditions. The P max at pH 3.5 (31.6 μW/cm2) was approximately 3.9 times and 9.9 times higher than that at pH 6.6 (6.5 μW/cm2) and pH 9.0 (2.9 μW/cm2), respectively.

In summary, with NCDs as electron transfer mediators, OPCN and Bi2O4 were combined to construct a Z-scheme heterojunction photocatalyst, namely OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4. With the help of the Z-scheme structure, the recombination of photo-generated electron-hole pairs was inhibited, and the charge carriers with high redox ability could be retained. Consequently, the photocatalytic performance with respect to activity and stability for the degradation of pollutants was significantly improved. Additionally, the PFC with OPCN/NCDs/Bi2O4 as the photoanode exhibited a greatly improved performance for decontamination and power generation compared with the PFC using single component (i.e. Bi2O4, OPCN or OPCN/NCDs) as the photoanode. This work provides an idea to design visible-light-responded photocatalysts for wastewater treatment.
Hongbin Yu: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yanan Xue: Investigation, Conceptualization. Shuang Liang: Validation. Xinhong Wang: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51878133).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114077.
